comments:
Summary:
This paper proposes Incremental Network Quantization (INQ), a method to convert pretrained full-precision CNNs into low-precision models whose weights are constrained to powers-of-two or zero. INQ alternates three components: (1) weight partition (pruning-inspired or random) to split weights into a group to be quantized and a group to remain floating-point, (2) group-wise quantization to a variable-length codebook of powers-of-two plus zero, and (3) re-training of the remaining floating-point weights while holding quantized weights fixed. The procedure is repeated incrementally until all weights are quantized. Experiments on ImageNet with AlexNet, VGG-16, GoogleNet and ResNets show that 5-bit quantization often matches or slightly improves full-precision accuracy; lower bit-widths (4/3/2 bits) are also explored. The paper additionally shows combinations with pruning/DNS to achieve high compression ratios compared to prior deep compression work.

Strengths:
- Practical, well-motivated approach: by quantizing subsets of weights and re-training the rest, the method addresses the typical accuracy loss of one-shot quantization strategies.
- Thorough empirical validation: experiments cover multiple network architectures on ImageNet, demonstrating robustness of the approach and good accuracy at 5-bit and even competitive results at lower bit-widths.
- Resulting representation (powers-of-two) is hardware-friendly (replace multiplies with shifts) and the variable-length encoding is sensible.
- Clear description of the algorithm (Algorithm 1) and reasonable implementation details (partition ratios, training epochs) are provided.

Weaknesses / Concerns:
- Limited analysis of computational/energy benefits on real hardware. While powers-of-two are attractive, the paper does not quantify actual runtime, memory bandwidth, or energy improvements on hardware (ASIC/FPGA/embedded CPUs). The claim that multiplies can be replaced by shifts would be strengthened by measurements or a microbenchmark.
- Some design choices are heuristic and not deeply justified: e.g., the formula for n1 (using factor 4/3), the specific accumulated quantization schedules, and chosen partition ratios. More ablation on sensitivity to these hyperparameters would be helpful.
- Comparisons to other recent quantization-from-scratch approaches (DoReFa-Net, QNN, XNOR-Net) are discussed qualitatively but could be made more quantitative (e.g., directly comparing top-1/top-5 on the same architectures when feasible). It is not always clear whether competing methods were tuned equivalently on ImageNet.
- The method focuses on quantizing pretrained models. It would be useful to clarify whether INQ can be applied during training from scratch (and how it compares in cost and performance), and report the extra training cost (wall-clock) compared with normal fine-tuning or other quantization-aware training methods.
- While results are impressive for 5-bit, accuracy drops at very low bit-widths (2-bit ternary) still exist; more discussion about practical tradeoffs and when these lower precisions are acceptable would help practitioners.

Suggestions for authors:
- Add an ablation study on (a) the sensitivity to partition schedules and partitioning strategy (beyond the random vs pruning-inspired comparison), and (b) the effect of the number of retraining epochs per iteration on final accuracy and training cost.
- Provide either hardware-level measurements (FPGA/CPU) or at least an estimate of theoretical speedups and memory savings to substantiate claims about deployment efficiency.
- If possible, include direct numeric comparisons to other recent quantization methods on ImageNet (same architectures and evaluation protocol), or explain limitations preventing such comparisons.
- Discuss limitations and future directions more explicitly: e.g., extending incremental strategy to activations and gradients, and limitations when starting from low-quality pretrained models.

Verdict:
This is a strong, practically useful contribution. INQ addresses an important shortcoming of many quantization methods by incrementally quantizing with re-training and demonstrates compelling ImageNet results across popular architectures. The experimental evaluation is extensive and supports the claims, though the paper would benefit from additional ablations and hardware/efficiency measurements. I recommend acceptance.
